current_fact.fact_text: In 1641, Guaraní warriors living in Jesuit reductions defeated Portuguese-backed bandeirantes at the Battle of Mbororé in present-day Argentina. Their victory slowed the large-scale enslavement raids into indigenous territories and showed how communities could unite across missions to defend their land.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

current_fact.match_reason: Both the Guaraní resistance and Tuareg music tell stories of indigenous defense, exile, and survival, making this desert-blues journey a fitting echo of South American struggles.

queue.fact_text: On the day Sebastián Piñera was sworn in as President of Chile in 2010, strong aftershocks from the Pichilemu earthquakes rattled the country during the ceremony. It was a visceral reminder that in Chile, one of Earth's most seismic boundaries, political transitions unfold on restless ground.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A political inauguration shaken by tectonic shifts pairs with an episode about movement, travel, and quiet internal quakes that alter perspective.

queue.fact_text: In 1927, showman Samuel "Roxy" Rothafel opened the Roxy Theatre in New York City, a movie palace so lavish it was nicknamed the "Cathedral of the Motion Picture." With a vast stage, a symphony orchestra, and cutting-edge sound, it turned filmgoing into a full-sensory spectacle.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The Roxy's grand opening as a 'cathedral' of entertainment mirrors Sedna FM’s own first, explosive broadcast — both are origin stories of immersive sound worlds.

queue.fact_text: The Great Blizzard of 1888 began on March 11 along the eastern seaboard of the United States, burying cities like New York and Boston under snowdrifts up to 15 meters high. Rail lines froze, telegraph wires snapped, and for days modern communication simply went quiet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A paralyzing blizzard that forced a fast-growing world to stop is well matched with a 'Slow Planet' soundtrack about enforced pauses and quieter rhythms.

queue.fact_text: In 1982, Widerøe Flight 933, a de Havilland Twin Otter, crashed into the icy Barents Sea near Gamvik, Norway, killing all 15 people aboard. The accident sparked years of investigation and controversy, with locals and families pressing for answers about what really happened in the Arctic dark.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Both the crash and the episode are set in Norway’s Arctic waters; the show's reflective, cinematic mood gently honors the sea’s beauty and its tragedies.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1941, U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed the Lend-Lease Act, authorizing vast shipments of weapons, food, and materials to Allied nations before the U.S. officially entered World War II. It transformed America into the "arsenal of democracy" and quietly tilted the balance of the war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Lend-Lease blurred strict national boundaries through supply lines, just as trip-hop blurred genre borders, creating new global flows of influence.

queue.fact_text: In 1945, the Imperial Japanese Navy launched Operation Tan No. 2, a massive kamikaze attack aimed at the U.S. Pacific Fleet anchored at Ulithi atoll. Pilots flew long distances in modified bombers, trying to turn their own aircraft into guided weapons in a last-ditch attempt to change the war’s course.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: The sudden, violent impact of kamikaze strikes is echoed metaphorically by the raw, high-voltage guitar energy of an 'Electric Shock' rock set.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1851, Giuseppe Verdi’s opera "Rigoletto" premiered in Venice, introducing audiences to a hunchbacked court jester whose tragic story scandalized censors. Its hit aria "La donna è mobile" was so catchy that the tenor was forbidden from singing it in public before opening night to prevent pirates from copying it.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: An iconic Italian opera debut fits with a reflective Italian-rooted episode; both capture how landscapes and language shape dramatic, melodic storytelling.

queue.fact_text: In 1845, during the Flagstaff War in New Zealand, chiefs Hōne Heke and Kawiti led Māori warriors in cutting down the British flagpole at Kororāreka for the fourth time. Their protest targeted not just wood and fabric but the very symbol of a treaty they felt had been mistranslated and misused.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: Māori defiance against imperial symbols resonates with a politically charged Rage Against the Machine deep-dive about protest, resistance, and reclaiming power.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 2018, a Bombardier Challenger 604 business jet crashed into the remote Zagros Mountains of Iran, killing all 11 passengers, many of them returning from a bachelorette celebration. The jet’s final descent turned a private, joyful journey into a stark reminder of how unforgiving mountain weather and terrain can be.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: A tragic crash in rugged mountains aligns with a reflective, sky-and-landscape focused episode that gently explores how terrain shapes fate and feeling.

queue.fact_text: In 1981, student protests erupted at the University of Pristina in Kosovo, then part of socialist Yugoslavia, as demonstrators demanded better conditions and greater political autonomy. What began as complaints about dorm life quickly expanded into a nationwide movement that reshaped Kosovo’s destiny.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: Students demanding more rights and identity align with an '(un)Boxing' theme about rejecting imposed labels and fighting for self-definition.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1387, at the Battle of Castagnaro, the English mercenary John Hawkwood led the forces of Padua to victory over Verona. He used a clever feigned retreat on marshy terrain, turning the battlefield into a trap that cemented his legend as one of Italy’s most feared condottieri.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: An Italian battlefield of strategy and power connects with an Italian-hosted rock episode about refusing permission and seizing the stage with loud, tactical confidence.

queue.fact_text: In 1343, Arnošt of Pardubice became the last Bishop of Prague and, a year later, the city’s first Archbishop, as the diocese was elevated in rank. His appointment signaled both religious reform and Prague’s rising importance as a spiritual and political center in medieval Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: An ecclesiastical elevation and the shaping of a religious center parallel an episode tracing the historical evolution of a profound spiritual-musical tradition.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 2023, the Burmese military carried out the Pinlaung massacre in Shan State, Myanmar, killing at least 30 villagers, including Buddhist monks sheltering civilians. The attack highlighted how even monasteries—traditionally seen as safe, sacred spaces—had become targets in the country’s ongoing conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A brutal attack on civilians and monks contrasts sharply with an episode about gentle persistence and learning; together they underline why societies must choose growth over violence.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 2020, the World Health Organization officially declared COVID-19 a pandemic, naming what millions of people were already feeling as the virus spread across continents. The single word "pandemic" shifted policies, borders, and daily routines almost overnight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: The global COVID-19 pandemic changed how we connect and rely on devices; an episode about our relationship with phones and disconnection resonates with that reshaped digital reality.

queue.fact_text: In 2006, Michelle Bachelet was inaugurated as Chile’s first female president, a former political prisoner and pediatrician breaking one of her country’s highest glass ceilings. Her election marked a powerful moment in South America’s ongoing redefinition of who holds power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: A pioneering woman head of state aligns perfectly with an episode celebrating powerful female voices who reshape culture and expectations.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1985, Mikhail Gorbachev became General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union, effectively its last leader. His policies of glasnost and perestroika loosened a rigid system—and helped set in motion the end of the USSR itself.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: Gorbachev’s reforms mixed openness and upheaval—fog and fire in a superpower—mirroring an episode themed around contrasting energies and deep structural change.

queue.fact_text: In 1977, the Hanafi Siege in Washington, D.C. saw around 150 hostages taken at multiple sites by gunmen seeking redress for perceived injustices against their community. The crisis ended only after ambassadors from three Muslim-majority nations helped negotiate a peaceful resolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A tense, politically charged hostage crisis resonates with an episode about Residente’s raw political critiques and the struggle for justice and identity.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 2008, Space Shuttle Endeavour’s STS-123 mission also delivered the Canadian-built Dextre robot to the ISS, a two-armed "handyman" designed to perform delicate maintenance in orbit. By fixing equipment outside the station, Dextre reduces the number of risky spacewalks astronauts have to make.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A nimble orbital robot doing intricate work in the dark pairs with a slow-building evening mix—starting mysterious, like a spacewalk, and blooming into rhythmic motion around a planet.

queue.fact_text: The 2011 Tōhoku earthquake subtly altered Earth’s rotation and shifted the planet’s axis by several centimeters, shortening the length of a day by a fraction of a second. Massive earthquakes don’t just move cities—they can make the whole planet wobble.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: A quake that literally imperfects Earth’s spin matches an episode celebrating wabi-sabi: the beauty in subtle shifts, flaws, and a planet that’s never perfectly still.

queue.fact_text: The Barents Sea, where Widerøe Flight 933 crashed, is also one of the world’s most biologically productive marginal seas, teeming with plankton, fish, and migratory whales under its icy surface. It’s a stark meeting point of human technology, Arctic weather, and ancient marine life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A cold, life-rich sea that hides both tragedy and abundance fits with an 'Immerse' episode about going below the surface and letting water-like music carry you.

queue.fact_text: The Treaty of Waitangi, whose mistranslations helped trigger the Flagstaff War, exists in multiple versions: the English text and the Māori text don’t fully match in how they describe sovereignty. Those linguistic gaps turned into real political fault lines that are still being debated today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #29 - Berber France Meets Arabic Italy
episode.description: Yasmin takes us back to her university years, where she met Abdel - an Algerian-French friend with a big heart and endless optimism. Their friendship grew around music, identity, and playful arguments about whether a song was originally Italian or French. Music that exists in both versions, alongside stories of finding a home, boxing training, and car rides filled with music.

queue.match_reason: A treaty fractured by translation is mirrored by an episode about songs that exist in multiple language versions—showing how words shift meaning across cultures.

published.fact_text: In 2008, Space Shuttle Endeavour lifted off on mission STS-123 carrying the first piece of Japan's Kibō laboratory to the International Space Station. Kibō, meaning "hope" in Japanese, became the largest single module on the ISS and Japan's primary orbital science outpost.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spaceflight milestone pairs naturally with an episode themed around 'Space Shift', both inviting listeners to see orbit—and emptiness itself—as a place for new beginnings.

published.fact_text: On March 11, 2011, a magnitude 9.0 megathrust earthquake struck off the coast of Japan, unleashing a devastating tsunami that crossed the Pacific. The disaster not only reshaped coastlines and communities, it also triggered the Fukushima nuclear accident, one of only two Level 7 events ever recorded.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A catastrophic wave and nuclear crisis need a reflective, weather-themed episode that speaks about storms, transitions, and the possibility of hope after devastation.

published.fact_text: In 1641, Guaraní warriors living in Jesuit reductions defeated Portuguese-backed bandeirantes at the Battle of Mbororé in present-day Argentina. Their victory slowed the large-scale enslavement raids into indigenous territories and showed how communities could unite across missions to defend their land.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Both the Guaraní resistance and Tuareg music tell stories of indigenous defense, exile, and survival, making this desert-blues journey a fitting echo of South American struggles.
